Part B.key_evidence: The central source is present in the science and difference cutouts as a compact residual, while there is no obvious bright point source exactly at the center of the template. The subtraction is negative, and ndethist is only 1 despite very large coverage (ncovhist 843). The science/template comparison suggests a positional offset or moving point source rather than repeated variability at a fixed persistent source.
Part B.leading_interpretation_and_support: This is most consistent with a real solar-system object (asteroid). The very low ndethist with high coverage, plus the need to explain a single-epoch detection, favors a mover. The cutouts do not show a clear host galaxy or persistent central source expected for SN/AGN, and the science-versus-template appearance is more compatible with an offset transient/moving source than with stationary astrophysical variability.
Part B.alternative_analysis: A bogus subtraction or a one-off stellar variability event is an alternative, since the cutouts are noisy and the residual is not extremely clean. However, the residual appears localized rather than a diffuse artifact, and the single-detection history makes a stationary variable or AGN less convincing than a solar-system interpretation.
